Source: https://github.com/bartezz32/firmware-arduino-nano-33-ble-sense
Snippet: bartezz32 / **firmware-arduino-nano-33-ble-sense** Public. forked from edgeimpulse/firmware-arduino-nano-33-ble-sense. Edge Impulse firmware for the Arduino Nano 33 BLE Sense development board. # bartezz32/firmware-arduino-nano-33-ble-sense. # Edge Impulse firmware for Arduino Nano 33 BLE Sense. This repository contains the Edge Impulse firmware for the Arduino Nano 33 BLE Sense development board. > **Note:** Do you just want to use this development board with Edge Impulse? * Arduino Nano 33 BLE Sense or Arduino Nano 33 BLE development board. * (Optional) Arduino Tiny Machine Learning Kit - to add sight to your Arduino Nano 33 BLE. The arduino-cli tool is used to build and upload the Edge Impulse firmware to the Arduino Nano 33 BLE Sense board. The Edge Impulse firmware depends on some libraries and the Mbed core for Arduino. * Arduino IDE (required for Windows users). *Installing Arduino IDE is a requirement only for Windows users. macOS and Linux users can use either the Arduino CLI or IDE to build the application.*. 1. Download and install the Arduino IDE for your Operating System. 2. In Tools -> Board -> Boards Manager, search for `nano 33` and install the package **Arduino Mbed OS Nano Boards v4.0.2**. ### Arduino IDE. 1. In Tools -> Board -> Boards Manager, search for `nano 33` and install the package **Arduino Mbed OS Boards v4.0.2**. 2. In Arduino Menu -> Preferences, check the location of the **preferences.txt** file (ie: /Users/aureleq/Library/Arduino15/). 3. Copy the `` file into the Arduino Mbed Nano 33 BLE Sense directory, for instance: `/Users/aureleq/Library/Arduino15/packages/arduino/hardware/mbed/4.0.2`. 4. Open the `firmware-arduino-nano-33-ble-sense.ino`, select the **Arduino nRF528x Boards (Mbed OS)** > **Arduino Nano 33 BLE** board. exit status 1 Error compiling for board Arduino Nano 33 BLE. Add the in your Arduino IDE application folder. * Failed to allocate TFLite arena (error code 1) / Failed to run impulse (-6). Failed to allocate TFLite arena (error code 1) Failed to run impulse (-6). In the case of (1) you may want to allocate the tensor arena statically by defining `"-DEI_CLASSIFIER_ALLOCATION_STATIC"` in `arduino-build.sh` or `` . If the problem still persists, then it may be that there's not enough RAM/heap for your model and this application. Edge Impulse firmware for the Arduino Nano 33 BLE Sense development board.

Source: https://github.com/badlogic/arduino-nano-esp32-esp-idf-template
Snippet: Title: GitHub - badlogic/arduino-nano-esp32-esp-idf-template: A template for ESP-IDF projects that target Arduino Nano ESP32 devices
badlogic   /  **arduino-nano-esp32-esp-idf-template**  Public template. A template for ESP-IDF projects that target Arduino Nano ESP32 devices. # badlogic/arduino-nano-esp32-esp-idf-template. # Arduino Nano ESP32 ESP-IDF template. This template makes it easy to set up an ESP-IDF project for Arduino Nano ESP32 including Arduino Core for ESP32 and VS Code support via the ESP-IDF VS Code extension. * Source-level debugging in VS Code via the built-in JTAG interface of the Arduino Nano ESP32. ### Installing ESP-IDF. ESP-IDF is Espressif's SDK for developing firmware for all their ESP32 SoCs. The simplest way to install it is via the VS Code extension. 1. Install the ESP-IDF VS Code extension. 2. Once installed, open the command palette in VS Code (`CTRL+P`/`CMD+P`) and run `ESP-IDF: Configure ESP-IDF extension`. You **MUST** use this version, as Arduino Core for ESP32 is not compatible with newer ESP-IDF versions. After installing and configuring the ESP-IDF VS Code extension, check if the folder `~/.espressif/tools/xtensa-esp32s3-elf` exists. I've filed an issue on the ESP-IDF VS Code extension repository. ### Preparing the Arduino Nano ESP32. ESP-IDF will not be able to flash or debug factory Nano ESP32s. 1. Connect your Arduino Nano ESP32 via USB to your computer. **The first time you flash it (as described in the next section), you have to manually press the reset button on the Nano ESP32 again for the flashed app to start. Subsequent flashing (and debugging) will not require you to press the reset button, as the ESP-IDF bootloader will already be installed and running.**. You can reset your Arduino Nano ESP32 to the factory bootloader as described here. 2. Clone the Git repository via `git clone https://github.com/badlogic/arduino-nano-esp32-esp-idf-template`. Then `git clone https://github.com//arduino-nano-esp32-esp-idf-template`. Once you've downloaded the template, open it in VS Code, and connect your Arduino Nano ESP32 via USB. * Build & flash: press `CTRL + SHIFT + B` (or `CMD + SHIFT + B` on macOS) to build and flash the app to your connected Arduino Nano ESP32. **If this is the first time you flash the app to your Nano ESP32, don't forget to press the reset button on the Nano!**. What you see on your Arduino Nano ESP32 is wrong. A template for ESP-IDF projects that target Arduino Nano ESP32 devices.
